lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: How goes the night, boy?

lines.speaker: FLEANCE
lines.text: The moon is down; I have not heard the clock.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: And she goes down at twelve.

lines.speaker: FLEANCE
lines.text: I take’t, ’tis later, sir.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Hold, take my sword. There’s husbandry in heaven;

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Their candles are all out. Take thee that too.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: A heavy summons lies like lead upon me,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: And yet I would not sleep: merciful powers,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Restrain in me the cursed thoughts that nature

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Gives way to in repose!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: A friend.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: What, sir, not yet at rest? The king’s a-bed:

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: He hath been in unusual pleasure, and

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Sent forth great largess to your offices.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: This diamond he greets your wife withal,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: By the name of most kind hostess; and shut up

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: In measureless content.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Being unprepared,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Our will became the servant to defect;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Which else should free have wrought.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: All’s well.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: I dreamt last night of the three weird sisters:

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: To you they have show’d some truth.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I think not of them:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Yet, when we can entreat an hour to serve,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: We would spend it in some words upon that business,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: If you would grant the time.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: At your kind’st leisure.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: If you shall cleave to my consent, when ’tis,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: It shall make honour for you.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: So I lose none

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: In seeking to augment it, but still keep

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: My bosom franchised and allegiance clear,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: I shall be counsell’d.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Good repose the while!

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: Thanks, sir: the like to you!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Go bid thy mistress, when my drink is ready,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: She strike upon the bell. Get thee to bed.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Is this a dagger which I see before me,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The handle toward my hand? Come, let me clutch thee.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I have thee not, and yet I see thee still.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Art thou not, fatal vision, sensible

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: To feeling as to sight? or art thou but

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: A dagger of the mind, a false creation,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Proceeding from the heat-oppressed brain?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I see thee yet, in form as palpable

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: As this which now I draw.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Thou marshall’st me the way that I was going;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And such an instrument I was to use.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Mine eyes are made the fools o’ the other senses,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Or else worth all the rest; I see thee still,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And on thy blade and dudgeon gouts of blood,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Which was not so before. There’s no such thing:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: It is the bloody business which informs

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Thus to mine eyes. Now o’er the one half-world

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Nature seems dead, and wicked dreams abuse

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The curtain’d sleep; witchcraft celebrates

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Pale Hecate’s offerings, and wither’d murder,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Alarum’d by his sentinel, the wolf,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Whose howl’s his watch, thus with his stealthy pace,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: With Tarquin’s ravishing strides, towards his design

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Moves like a ghost. Thou sure and firm-set earth,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Hear not my steps, which way they walk, for fear

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Thy very stones prate of my whereabout,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And take the present horror from the time,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Which now suits with it. Whiles I threat, he lives:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Words to the heat of deeds too cold breath gives. A bell rings.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I go, and it is done; the bell invites me.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Hear it not, Duncan; for it is a knell

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: That summons thee to heaven or to hell.

stage_directions.text: Enter Banquo, and Fleance bearing a torch before him.
Enter Macbeth, and a Servant with a torch.
Give me my sword. Who’s there?

glosses.definition: the moon sets at midnight
economy in heaven — the stars have been 'saved' like candles
a sleepiness as weighty as an arrest warrant
sinful thoughts that come in sleep
generous gifts to the household staff
with it
Duncan's courtesy, expressed moments before his murder — the dramatic irony is unbearable
free; unfettered
unstained; pure

glosses.phrase: Is this a dagger which I see before me
glosses.definition: Macbeth's famous vision — the air forms a dagger pointing toward Duncan's chamber; the reality of the weapon is the play's core epistemological question
the handle turned toward my grasp
a vision that announces death; a prophecy
the dagger may be a product of his own mind — Macbeth's self-interrogation
a deceptive fabrication
a brain overheated by anxious thought — fever was believed to cause hallucinations
drops of blood; the dagger begins to appear bloodied
drops; large splashes
takes sensory shape; forms itself visibly
the half of the globe currently in night
Greek goddess of witchcraft, crossroads, and the moon; patron of the Weird Sisters

glosses.phrase: Alarum’d by his sentinel, the wolf
glosses.definition: woken by his own watchdog, the wolf — whose howl signals the hour
silent, creeping step
like Tarquin, the Roman rapist of Lucrece — silent advance toward a bed
solid ground — Macbeth begs it not to report his steps

glosses.phrase: very stones prate of my whereabout
glosses.definition: the stones may tell tales of where I am
funeral bell — the signal Lady Macbeth will ring

annotations.title: The dagger and the epistemology of guilt
annotations.body:
'Is this a dagger which I see before me, / The handle toward my hand?' The soliloquy's grammar is an interrogation. Macbeth asks the vision for its credentials: is it real, is it a 'dagger of the mind,' is it a 'false creation / Proceeding from the heat-oppressed brain?' The play will not answer. The dagger may be objective (a demonic apparition), subjective (a guilty hallucination), or both at once.

The uncertainty is the point. *Macbeth* is a play about the interpretability of the world — the witches whose prophecies are true but deceptive, the faces that cannot be read (1.4), the ghost that only Macbeth sees (3.4). The dagger scene stages the epistemological crisis in one long speech: a man cannot trust what his senses report, and must nevertheless act on what they show him.

Macbeth resolves the question by declaring 'there's no such thing' and proceeding anyway. The dagger has done its work: real or imagined, it has pointed him at the bedroom. Whether matter causes mind or mind causes matter, the deed happens either way.

annotations.title: Night as accomplice
annotations.body:
The second half of the soliloquy summons a night of murder: 'Now o'er the one half-world / Nature seems dead, and wicked dreams abuse / The curtain'd sleep.' Hecate, witchcraft, the wolf, 'withered Murder' like Tarquin — an iconography of the classical and folk supernatural all gathered to assist him.

The speech rhymes with Lady Macbeth's 'blanket of the dark' in 1.5 and with Macbeth's 'Stars, hide your fires' in 1.4. The three invocations build: where she asked for cover, he now asks for assistance. 'Thou sure and firm-set earth, / Hear not my steps.' The earth itself is being drafted into the conspiracy.

The Tarquin reference is precise. Tarquin — whose rape of Lucrece Shakespeare told in his earlier narrative poem — is the play's analogue for this night: a silent approach to a sleeping victim's bed, ending in a death that will outlast the perpetrator.
sources: Catherine Belsey, *Shakespeare and the Loss of Eden* (1999)

trials.prompt: The dagger soliloquy refuses to resolve whether the dagger is:
options: Real (supernatural) or imagined (hallucination) — the play leaves both possibilities open

trials.prompt: Macbeth compares his approach to Duncan's chamber to:
options: Tarquin's ravishing strides — the Roman rapist's silent advance toward a sleeping bed
Declaring 'there's no such thing' and proceeding anyway — acting without settling whether the vision is real

trials.prompt: Duncan's closing line in 2.1 ('goes off' to bed peacefully thanking Macbeth for his hospitality) functions as:
options: Unbearable dramatic irony — Duncan's kindness to his host is delivered moments before his murder
